premise.title: Relation between coherence length and upper critical field

premises.premise: The coherence length of the superconducting order parameter, denoted by $\xi$, is determined from the upper critical field $H_{c2}$ using the relation: $\xi = \sqrt{\frac{\phi_{0}}{3\pi^{2}H_{c2}}}$ where $\phi_{0}$ is the magnetic flux quantum.

The Fermi velocity $v_{F}$ is related to the coherence length $\xi$ and the critical temperature $T_{c}$ via the BCS relation: $\xi = 0.18 \frac{h v_{F}}{k_{\mathrm{B}} T_{c}}$ where $h$ is the Planck constant (notated as $h$ in the text) and $k_{\mathrm{B}}$ is the Boltzmann constant.

assumption.title: Validity of WHH model for upper critical field estimation

premises.assumption: The temperature dependence of the upper critical field $H_{c2}$ can be approximated by the Werthamer–Helfand–Hohenberg (WHH) model for all temperatures to estimate $H_{c2}$ at zero temperature. 45

step.title: Experimental determination of critical temperature and field

reasoning.step: The authors measure the superconducting transition temperature $T_c$ and the dependence of $T_c$ on the applied external magnetic field for $YH_6$ and $YD_9$ samples.

step.title: Estimation of Hc2(0) using WHH model

reasoning.step: The authors fit the experimental values of $H_{c2}(T)$ to the Werthamer–Helfand–Hohenberg (WHH) model [@assumption-3]. These fits yield $H_{c2}^{\mathrm{WHH}}(0\mathrm{K})=157\mathrm{T}$ for $hcc-YH_{6}$ and $H_{c2}^{\mathrm{WHH}}(0\mathrm{K})=60\mathrm{T}$ for $hcp-YD_{9}$.

Using the estimated $H_{c2}(0\mathrm{K})$ values, the authors calculate the coherence length $\xi(0\mathrm{K})$ for each phase using the relation $\xi = \sqrt{\frac{\phi_{0}}{3\pi^{2}H_{c2}}}$ [@premise-1].
The authors calculate the Fermi velocities $v_{F}$ using the BCS relation $\xi = 0.18 \frac{h v_{F}}{k_{\mathrm{B}} T_{c}}$ [@premise-2], substituting the calculated $\xi$ and measured $T_c$.

The calculation yields $v_F = 2.3 \times 10^{5}$ m/s for $YH_{6}$ and $v_F = 2.85 \times 10^{5}$ m/s for $YD_{9}$. The authors observe that despite the substantial difference in $H_{c2}$ (157 T vs 60 T), the Fermi velocities are quite similar.

Based on the similarity of the calculated Fermi velocities, the authors infer that the dispersion of the charge carriers contributing to superconductivity does not significantly change between these different superconducting hydride phases.

inference_unit.conclusion: The authors calculate the Fermi velocities $v_{F}$ for the superconducting phases using the BCS relation $\xi = 0.18 \frac{\hbar v_{F}}{k_{\mathrm{B}} T_{c}}$. The estimated values are $2.3 \times 10^{5}$ m/s for $YH_{6}$ and $2.85 \times 10^{5}$ m/s for $YD_{9}$, indicating that the dispersion of charge carriers is similar across these hydride phases despite differences in $H_{c2}$.
